text: Hi, I'm Si, ski and snowboard coach here at Alpine Snow Sports.
A lot of people ask us about the differences between skiing on a dry slope and skiing on snow.
So we've prepared the following video to help you get the most out of a dry slope.
Alpine Snow Sports
The great thing about dry slopes is they're inexpensive, they're very accessible,
but more importantly they provide a consistent surface to train on.
Now if you're coming to ski on a dry slope for the first time and you've already skied on snow,
there are two principles that can really help you get the most out of a dry slope.
The first one is to keep your feet underneath your body.
Don't be too edgy in your performance to start with.
A good way to practice this is to start off in a snow plow
until you can feel yourself balancing against your outside ski.
From there you'll easily progress into parallel turns.
Alpine Snow Sports
The second thing that can really help,
when you're on a dry slope,
is to make sure you're balanced over the centre of your foot.
A good way of checking this is to do some straight running
right at the bottom of the hill
and do a few small jumps or hops on the way down.
If the whole ski is coming off the ground,
we know that you're centred.
You can then progress and put this into your parallel turns
to ensure that when the going gets tough,
you're still balanced.

The most important thing to remember with a dry slope
is because your movements need to be more refined
it's an incredibly good way of training before you go out on snow.

you'll be king of the hill on snow.
